content.text: An interface in TypeScript is a named contract that describes the shape of an object: the properties it must have, their types, and the methods it must expose. Like a type alias for an object, an interface has no runtime representation — it is erased during compilation. The key distinction is that interfaces support declaration merging and can be used in extends clauses, making them the preferred choice for extensible object shapes.

attrs.alt: Interface anatomy diagram showing properties, optional, readonly, methods, and index signatures
attrs.title: Interface Anatomy

content.text: Interface properties can be marked optional (?) or read-only (readonly), narrowing the contract to match real-world requirements.
Interfaces can describe methods in three equivalent syntaxes. The differences matter for strictness settings and for how TypeScript handles covariant overrides.
An index signature allows an interface to describe an object with dynamic property keys. It is used for dictionaries, maps, and API responses with variable fields.
The extends keyword lets one interface inherit all members of another, adding new properties. Multiple inheritance is supported — a single interface can extend several others.
A class uses the implements keyword to declare that it satisfies an interface. TypeScript verifies that every required member exists with a compatible type. A class can implement multiple interfaces.

TypeScript merges multiple interface declarations with the same name into a single combined interface. This is the main reason to prefer interface over type for library extension points — a type alias with the same name would be a duplicate error.
Structural Typing and Excess Property Checks

TypeScript uses structural typing: an object satisfies an interface as long as it has at least the required properties, regardless of its origin. However, object literals trigger excess property checks — passing extra properties directly in a literal is flagged as an error.

Interfaces accept type parameters, allowing them to describe shapes that work with multiple concrete types. Generic interfaces are common in Angular for service contracts, HTTP wrappers, and observable patterns.
An interface can describe a function or a constructor by including a call signature or a construct signature. This is how TypeScript types higher-order functions and factory patterns.

Both interfaces and abstract classes can define contracts for implementations. The key difference is that abstract classes can include implementation details and are emitted as JavaScript; interfaces are type-only and are erased at runtime.
No — erased, cannot be used as token
Yes — class reference works as DI token

Interfaces define the shape of objects in TypeScript. They support optional and readonly properties, method signatures, index signatures, extension via extends, and class implementation via implements. Declaration merging makes them the extension point of choice for augmenting third-party types.
for object shapes that may be extended, merged, or implemented by a class.
Method shorthand is bivariant; property-function syntax is contravariant — prefer property-function for stricter callback types.
is cleaner for typed key sets.
A class can implement multiple interfaces; TypeScript verifies every required member.
Declaration merging — unique to interfaces — allows safe augmentation of external types without modifying source.
Structural typing means shape compatibility, not name equality. Object literals trigger stricter excess property checks.
